title: Promoting Engagement Through the Optimization of Digital Just-in-Time Adaptive Health Interventions

summary.hook: This poster describes a micro-randomized trial (MRT) feasibility study aimed at optimizing a just-in-time adaptive intervention (JITAI) to increase wear time of a wrist-worn alcohol biosensor (Skyn) among young adults with HIV.
summary.summary_30s: This poster describes a micro-randomized trial (MRT) feasibility study aimed at optimizing a just-in-time adaptive intervention (JITAI) to increase wear time of a wrist-worn alcohol biosensor (Skyn) among young adults with HIV. The team tested engagement strategies at onboarding (standard vs engagement), using reciprocity rewards and personalized feedback delivered through a gamified smartphone app plus message strategy, while collecting daily ecological momentary assessments (EMAs). Preliminary results show 20% overall attrition after baseline and substantially higher attrition in the standard condition (37%) than in the engagement condition (17%); retention was associated with several person-level factors including income, sensation-seeking, mental health symptom severity, and extraversion.
talktrack_2m: Focus: Develop and optimize an engagement-focused JITAI to increase wear time of the Skyn alcohol biosensor among young adults with HIV.
Approach: Population: Young adults (18–29) with HIV; baseline sample n=49. Primary outcome: Proximal: Skyn biosensor wear time within last 12 hours.
Finding: Baseline sample: n=49 young adults (18–29) with HIV completed baseline session.

Why it matters: Authors’ stated conclusions: Retention is lower without engagement strategies; person-level factors are associated with retention; MRT and usability analyses are ongoing. Implications (poster-grounded): Adding engagement strategies at onboarding (gamified app + message strategy) may reduce attrition in studies requiring ongoing wearable use among young adults with HIV. Practical barriers (unstable housing, financial concerns, device difficulties) can undermine participation and need to be addressed in design and compensation structures. Mechanism/explanation (ONLY if stated): Reciprocity leverages a tendency to return favors; personalized feedback increases awareness and provides a concrete improvement target. * Connections to prior work (ONLY if referenced): Not stated on poster (no citations listed beyond funding and ClinicalTrials.gov).
key_numbers: Proximal wear-time outcome assessed in last 12 hours; distal outcome assessed at 1-month follow-up; an MRT diagram notes daily randomizations “for 30 days.”
Young adults (18–29) with HIV; baseline sample n=49.
Baseline n=49; onboarding conditions: Standard 69% vs Engagement 31%.
Attrition higher in Standard (37%) than Engagement (17%).
takeaways: Baseline sample: n=49 young adults (18–29) with HIV completed baseline session.
Onboarding condition distribution: Standard 69% vs Engagement 31%.
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: What was the exact operational definition of “wear time within last 12 hours” (thresholds, missingness rules)?

what_to_ask.question: Attrition was 37% in Standard vs 17% in Engagement—what do you think drove the improvement (app, messages, rewards, or combination)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: Did you adjust for multiple comparisons in the retention factor analyses, or treat these as exploratory?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: If a clinic wanted to deploy this, what is the minimal viable engagement package (reciprocity, feedback, gamification, or a subset)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.
